item.title: Answering The Age Old Question of Which Role Has The Most Impact
item.dc:creator: Kavanaugh Splittgerber
item.description:
League of legends is a team focused esport. This should not be shocking or anything like that, everyone knows how much team work matters in League. One player falling behind can set the whole team back whole side objectives worth of progress. Games are lost on players falling behind, and games are won on every player pulling their weight. However, League of legends is also well known for being a complicated game, part of this is because of how different the five roles are. So with every player being very important, and every player playing a completely different role, this naturally leads to everyone’s competitive spirit coming out. So the question arises, which role is the most important in a match? Out of the five roles, which one holds the most impact? First things first, in order to determine which role has the most impact, looking at individual role stats is most important, primary and secondary objectives are tied to the team as a whole so they aren’t helpful here. Second, The League of Legends esport is very large with lots of players ranging across many skill levels, so keep the playing field as even as possible, only players who participated at Worlds 2025 will be looked at. So, looking at performance for each role.

Code 
library(tidyverse)
library(ggrepel)
library(gt)
library(wehoop)
library(patchwork)
worlds25 <- read_csv("Worlds2025clean.csv")

worldstop <- worlds25 |>
  filter(Position == "TOP")

worldsjng <- worlds25 |>
  filter(Position == "JNG")

worldsmid <- worlds25 |>
  filter(Position == "MID")

worldsadc <- worlds25 |>
  filter(Position == "ADC")

worldssup <- worlds25 |>
  filter(Position == "SUP")

ggplot() +
  geom_point(
    data=worlds25,
    aes(x=AvgKills, y=AvgDeaths, size=AvgAssists)
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~Position) +
labs(
    x="Average kills per game", 
    y="Average deaths per game",
    size="Average assists per game",
    title="How well each player from each role performed per game", 
    subtitle="Tracking every role's ability to play as team, play on their own, and stay alive.", 
    caption="Source: Games of Legends eSports | By Kavanaugh Splittgerber"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() +
   theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 17, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )

The three key stats to look at are kills, deaths, and assists. This looks at how good each role is at playing on its own, playing with the team, and staying alive. Some obvious things to point out, Support and Jungle have lots of assists, and Support has very few kills across the board as well. These roles are both much more focused on playing with the team and enabling them to do well, so their stats are naturally not too impressive to see. The big outlier here is ADC, they have a great spread with lots of kills and few deaths. But, ADCs spend the most time out of everyone on their team playing with the Support, so how many of their kills are due to their own role and not Support?

Code 
killers <- worlds25 |>
  group_by(Position) |>
  summarize(
    total = sum(SoloKills)
  )

rows <- 10

worldwaffle <- killers |>
  uncount(total) |>
  mutate(
    x = (row_number() - 1) %/% rows + 1,
    y = (row_number() - 1) %% rows + 1
  ) |>
  ungroup()


ggplot() +
  geom_tile(
    data=worldwaffle,
    aes(
      x = x, y = y, fill = Position
      ),
    color = "white",
    linewidth = .5) +
  coord_equal() +
  theme_void() +
  labs(
    x = "1 square = 1 solo kill",
    title="Top and mid laners perform great when alone", 
    subtitle="A solo kill requires no other teammates to be present, which some roles depend on.",
    caption="Source: Game of Legends eSports | Graphic by Kavanaugh Splittgerber",
    fill = "Record") + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8),
    axis.title.y = element_blank(),
    strip.text.x = element_text(size = 6, hjust=0)
  )

Well well well, when we look at solo kills, ADCs look a lot less impressive. Obviously Jungle and Support still don’t look great as they are team players first and independent players second. Top and Mid spend the most time in 1v1 scenarios compared to other roles so when looking at how good they perform independently, it puts them back in the running with ADCs. Kills are obviously important, but how much effort it takes to earn a kill is also something to consider. A squishy Support is going to be a lot easier to take down then a tanky Top laner, so a solo kill against a weak target is a lot less impressive. Luckily, tracking the amount of effort each role puts into earning kills is something that can be tracked.

Code 
adct1 <- worldsadc |> filter(Player == "Gumayusi")
  
bar4 <- ggplot() +
  geom_bar(data=worldsadc, aes(x=Player, weight=DPM)) +
  geom_bar(data=adct1, aes(x=Player, weight=DPM), fill="red") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    x="Player",
    y="Average damage per minute") +
  theme_minimal()
  

topt1 <- worldstop |> filter(Player == "Doran")
  
bar1 <- ggplot() +
  geom_bar(data=worldstop, aes(x=Player, weight=DPM)) +
  geom_bar(data=topt1, aes(x=Player, weight=DPM), fill="red") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    x="Player",
    y="Average damage per minute") +
  theme_minimal()


jngt1 <- worldsjng |> filter(Player == "Oner")

bar2 <- ggplot() +
  geom_bar(data=worldsjng, aes(x=Player, weight=DPM)) +
  geom_bar(data=jngt1, aes(x=Player, weight=DPM), fill="red") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    x="Player",
    y="Average damage per minute") +
  theme_minimal()


midt1 <- worldsmid |> filter(Player == "Faker")

bar3 <- ggplot() +
  geom_bar(data=worldsmid, aes(x=Player, weight=DPM)) +
  geom_bar(data=midt1, aes(x=Player, weight=DPM), fill="red") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    x="Player",
    y="Average damage per minute") +
  theme_minimal()


supt1 <- worldssup |> filter(Player =="Keria")

bar5 <- ggplot() +
  geom_bar(data=worldssup, aes(x=Player, weight=DPM)) +
  geom_bar(data=supt1, aes(x=Player, weight=DPM), fill="red") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    x="Player",
    y="Average damage per minute") +
  theme_minimal()

bar1 + bar2 + bar3 + bar4 + bar5  + 
  plot_annotation(
    title = "ADC brings the damage in every game",
    subtitle = "Great ADCs are even over double the average damage per minute of other roles.",
    caption = "Source: Game of Legends eSports | By Kavanaugh Splittgerber"
  ) & 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )

Who is pulling the most weight? It’s the ADCs. So even though ADCs don’t get the most solo kills, they are still putting in the majority of effort when they do get kills. So in conclusion, ADC is definitely the most impactful role in League of Legend. ADCs may need support to do the best they can do, but without an ADC, the team loses their biggest threat. The team’s playmaker is the ADC which sadly means that all of the ADC players who act like they are the most important member of the team might be right.

item.title: Is the Torpedo Bat the Game Changer Hitters Needed
item.dc:creator: Kavanaugh Splittgerber
item.description:
In the recent seasons in the MLB, batting average across the whole league was hitting record lows, and was only getting lower. Pitching has gotten better and better over the years as pitchers have improved and developed new techniques, causing batting to head on a downward spiral as pitching numbers only went up. Enter Aaron Leanhardt, a hitting coordinator for the New York Yankees who started working with the Yankee hitters to see what could be done to shift the scales in an era where pitching is what rules games. Working with the team’s hitters, Aaron eventually came up with the bowling pin bat, or torpedo bat, which had the majority of weight within the bat focused around 7 inches down from the tip, rather than at the tip like a regular bat. The bat was fully realized and used by the New York Yankees in the 2025 season where they had an extremely promising start, hitting 15 home runs in the team’s first three game series of the season. The introduction of this new bat sparked a lot of controversy but the bat was never banned as it followed all the necessary rules to be considered a legal bat. So now that the torpedo bat’s debut season is over, let’s look at how the bat changed the game and whether or not the tornado bat really did even the playing field. First things first, the torpedo bat was invited for one primary purpose, to let hitters hit the damn ball better. And in baseball, hitting the ball better means hitting it farther and more often. Starting with hitting the ball farther, no better metric exists than looking at home runs, hitting the ball the farthest.

Code 
library (tidyverse)
players24 <- read_csv("mlbplayerseason24.csv") |>
  mutate(Player=gsub("\\*", "",Player)) |>
  mutate(Player=gsub("\\#", "",Player))

nyyplayers24 <- players24 |>
  filter(Team=="NYY", G > 2)

players25 <- read_csv("mlbplayerseason25.csv") |>
  mutate(Player=gsub("\\*", "",Player)) |>
  mutate(Player=gsub("\\#", "",Player))

activeplayers25 <- players25 |>
  filter(H > 1)

nyyplayers25 <- players25 |>
  filter(Team=="NYY", G > 2)

teams25 <- read_csv("mlbteamseason25.csv")

yankees25 <- teams25 |>
  filter(Tm == "New York Yankees")

teams24 <- read_csv("mlbteamseason24.csv")

yankees24 <- teams24 |>
  filter(Tm == "New York Yankees")

pitching24 <- read_csv("mlbpitching24.csv") |> mutate(season = 2024)

pitching25 <- read_csv("mlbpitching25.csv") |> mutate(season = 2025)

pitchingcombined <- bind_rows(pitching24, pitching25)

pitchingdumbbell <- pitchingcombined |>
  select(Tm, season, WHIP) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from=season, values_from=WHIP)

ggplot() +
  geom_bar(data=teams25, aes(x=reorder(Tm, HR), weight=HR)) +
  geom_bar(data=yankees25, aes(x=reorder(Tm,HR), weight=HR), fill="navyblue") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    x="Team",
    y="Home runs",
    title="Yankees hold a strong lead on Home runs",
    subtitle="Introduction of the torpedo bat led to the Yankees rising far above the other teams in home runs.",
    caption="Source: Sports Reference | Kavanaugh Splittgerber"
  ) +
theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10)
  )

Yes the New York Yankees are good at hitting the ball the farthest. During the 2025 season they had an impressive lead in homeruns. Not only that, but their home run tally was the highest it’s been for many seasons. But how much of a difference did the torpedo bat make?

Code 
ggplot() +
  geom_bar(data=teams24, aes(x=reorder(Tm, HR), weight=HR)) +
  geom_bar(data=yankees24, aes(x=reorder(Tm,HR), weight=HR), fill="navyblue") +
  coord_flip() +
   labs(
    x="Team",
    y="Home runs",
    title="Yankees didn't struggle to hit home runs",
    subtitle="The 2024 season had a low batting average, but home runs weren't rare.",
    caption="Source: Sports Reference | Kavanaugh Splittgerber"
  ) +
theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10)
  )

Looking at the season right before the torpedo bat was introduced, the Yankees are still leading in home runs. The tally is a lot closer, and the Yankees still hit many more home runs with the torpedo bat than without it, but coming into the 2024 season, the Yankees’ hitters were already upping their game. How much of the Yankees success is thanks to the torpedo bat rather than just their hitters playing at their best. Next let’s look at the second part of hitting the damn ball better, hitting it more often. After all, the whole reason the torpedo bat was invented was because batting average was going down.

Code 
ggplot() + 
  geom_point(data=nyyplayers24, aes(x=AB, y=H, color="season 2024")) +
  geom_point(data=nyyplayers25, aes(x=AB, y=H, color="season 2025")) +
  scale_color_manual(values=c("red", "navyblue")) +
  labs(
    x="Times up to bat",
    y="Hits",
    title="Seasons with and without the torpedo bat",
    subtitle="The new torpedo bat did have a positive effect on the Yankees' accuracy, but not a large one.",
    caption="Source: Sports Reference | Kavanaugh Splittgerber"
  ) +
theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10)
  )

It’s tough to decide whether or not the torpedo bat made a difference. There may be more red dots up in the right corner which looks good, but for this graph, being higher is more important and what we are looking at rather than seeing how far right a dot is. Overall the Yankees in the 2025 season had less times up to bat so naturally they had less hits. But how often they hit was better, but not by a crazy amount.

Code 
ggplot() + 
  geom_point(data=activeplayers25, aes(x=AB, y=H), color="grey") +
  geom_point(data=nyyplayers25, aes(x=AB, y=H), color="navyblue") +
  labs(
    x="Times up to bat",
    y="Hits",
    title="Yankees are hitting, but so is everyone else",
    subtitle="The Yankees had a great accuracy when swingging, but they don't particularly stand out.",
    caption="Source: Sports Reference | Kavanaugh Splittgerber"
  ) +
theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10)
  )

It doesn’t get any better when looking at the Yankees compared to the other teams during the 2025 season. Yes there players have a very solid batting average across the board, but this isn’t exclusive to the Yankees. They have more hitters above the league average than below, but they don’t have any particularly great standouts. So they are doing good, but maybe not as good as you would expect for using a new supposed game changing bat. Now the torpedo bat was introduced because of low batting averages, but the reason why batting averages were so low was because pitching got so good. Pitchers were great at stopping hitters from gaining bases, so if the torpedo bat did its job, then it would have made it harder for pitchers to prevent bases.

Code 
ggplot() +
  geom_segment(
    data=pitchingdumbbell,
    aes(
      y=Tm, yend=Tm,
      x=`2024`, xend=`2025`)
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data=pitchingdumbbell,
    aes(
      y=Tm,
      x=`2024`,
      color="2024"
    ),
    size=2.5
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data=pitchingdumbbell,
    aes(
      y=Tm,
      x=`2025`,
      color="2025"
    ),
    size=2.5
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(name="Season", values=c("red", "navyblue")
  ) +
   labs(
    x="Walks and Hits per inning pitched",
    y="Team",
    title="Pitchers are letting up more bases",
    subtitle="In the 2025 season, more teams had pitchers giving more hits and walks to hitters.",
    caption="Source: Sports Reference | Kavanaugh Splittgerber"
  ) +
theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10)
  )

And the torpedo bat did its job pretty well! The majority of teams had pitchers giving up more bases in the 2025 season than the 2024 season. Some teams even had quite the change from season to season while others stayed relatively the same, or even got better at keeping hitters off bases. So is the torpedo bat the game changer it was supposed to be. The data shows that yes, it did have an impact on performance. Not only that, but it even brought down pitching performance for the first time in a while. However, the torpedo bat isn’t a miracle tool. Teams that used the torpedo bat performed better than they used to but teams that didn’t pick it up didn’t fall miles behind. It also wasn’t impossible for pitchers to still perform well and do their job against the new bat. So the torpedo bat is effective and succeeds in its goal but not to a massive extent. But any improvement is an improvement so we will probably see more players use the torpedo bat in the future. And if the stats stay looking good, it may even become the new standard.

item.title: Post With Code
item.dc:creator: Harlow Malloc
item.description:
This is a post with executable code.

1 + 1
[1] 2

item.title: Welcome To My Blog
item.dc:creator: Tristan O'Malley
item.description:
This is the first post in a Quarto blog. Welcome!

Since this post doesn’t specify an explicit image, the first image in the post will be used in the listing page of posts.
